---
title: "Infrastructure"
author: David Kane
tutorial:
  id: "09-infrastructure"
output:
  learnr::tutorial:
    df_print: default
    progressive: true
    allow_skip: true
runtime: shiny_prerendered
description: "A tour of devcontainers and how they define reproducible development environments in GitHub Codespaces. Covers the devcontainer.json file, base images, VS Code extensions, and lifecycle commands."
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
library(learnr)
library(tutorial.helpers)
library(knitr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(out.width = '90%')
options(tutorial.exercise.timelimit = 60,
        tutorial.storage = "local")
```

```{r info-section, child = system.file("child_documents/info_section.Rmd", package = "tutorial.helpers")}
```

## Introduction
###

This tutorial explores devcontainers --- configuration files that define a reproducible development environment in GitHub Codespaces. You will examine a `devcontainer.json` file to understand how it specifies the base image, installed packages, VS Code extensions, and startup commands that make a Codespace ready to use the moment it opens. You will also compare two different Codespace environments side by side.

### Exercise 1

You must do this tutorial in a Codespace created from a GitHub repo named `09-infrastructure`. If you haven't already done this, follow these steps:

1. Create a GitHub Repo: go to the [codespace-starter repo](https://github.com/ppbds-student/codespace-starter). Click the green "Use this Template" drop-down button and select "Create a new repository". Name the repo `09-infrastructure`. Then click "Create repository".

2. Create a Codespace from `09-infrastructure`: from this repo's page, click the green "Code" drop down button, select the "Codespaces" tab, and click "Create Codespace on main".

Copy and paste the URL for your `09-infrastructure` repository.

```{r introduction-1}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

Your answer should look something like:

````
https://github.com/ppbds-student/09-infrastructure
````

###

The `codespace-starter` repo includes a `.devcontainer/devcontainer.json` file that tells GitHub exactly what to install when a Codespace starts. This configuration is what makes the environment reproducible --- anyone who opens a Codespace from the same repo gets the same tools, extensions, and settings.

### Exercise 2

Copy and paste the URL for your Codespace.

```{r introduction-2}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

Your answer should look something like:

````
https://congenial-chainsaw-wv757pv4pp67cpw.github.dev/
````

###

Every Codespace runs on a virtual machine in the cloud. The URL you just pasted is a unique address for your specific machine --- a different person opening a Codespace from the same repo would get a different URL, but the same environment.

### Exercise 3

At the Terminal, run `pwd`, then `ls -a`. CP/CR.

```{r introduction-3}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 6)
```

###

Your answer should look like:

````
@ppbds-student ➜ /workspaces/09-infrastructure (main) $ pwd
/workspaces/09-infrastructure
@ppbds-student ➜ /workspaces/09-infrastructure (main) $ ls -a
.  ..  .devcontainer  .git
@ppbds-student ➜ /workspaces/09-infrastructure (main) $
````

###

The `.devcontainer` directory is what tells GitHub Codespaces how to configure this environment. Without it, GitHub would use a generic default image with no customization. The presence of `.git` confirms this is a version-controlled repository.

### Exercise 4

Run `cat .devcontainer/devcontainer.json`. CP/CR.

```{r introduction-4}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 15)
```

###

Your output will show the full `devcontainer.json` for the `codespace-starter` template. You will explore each part of this file in the Devcontainers section. Keep this Codespace open --- you will need it alongside a second Codespace in the next section.

## Devcontainers
###

A **devcontainer** is a configuration-as-code approach to development environments. Instead of documenting setup steps in a README and hoping every collaborator follows them correctly, a `devcontainer.json` file encodes the entire environment --- base image, packages, extensions, and startup commands --- so that any Codespace opened from the repo starts in an identical, ready-to-use state.

### Exercise 1

Go to https://github.com/codespaces/templates. Then press the "Use This Template" button for the Jupyter Notebook notebook template. This will open a new Codespace using this template. Copy and paste its URL.

```{r}
include_graphics("images/jupyter-template.png")
```

```{r devcontainers-1}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

The GitHub Jupyter Notebook template is a pre-built Codespace designed for Python data science work using Jupyter notebooks. Python is another powerful language for doing data science and Jupyter notebooks, like Quarto documents, contain text, code, and visualizations. The `codespace-starter` template we've been using, on the other hand, is configured specifically for R, Quarto, and running tutorials.

### Exercise 2

Now, you should have two Codespaces open. Go to https://github.com/codespaces. Then copy and paste the two table rows corresponding to your open Codespaces.

```{r}
include_graphics("images/open-codespaces.png")
```

```{r devcontainers-2}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

The github.com/codespaces page is where you manage all your active environments. Each Codespace runs on a virtual machine that consumes compute hours from your GitHub quota --- free accounts receive a monthly allowance. Stop or delete Codespaces you're not using to conserve it.

### Exercise 3

Select the `devcontainer.json` file within the `.devcontainer` directory in the File Explorer. Find the line beginning with "image", copy and paste.

```{r devcontainers-3}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

Your answer should look like this:

```
"image": "mcr.microsoft.com/devcontainers/universal:2",
```

###

The `image` field specifies the pre-built environment your Codespace starts from --- think of it as a snapshot of an operating system with software already installed. `mcr.microsoft.com/devcontainers/universal:2` is Microsoft's "Universal" snapshot, which includes Python, Node.js, Go, and other common languages out of the box.

### Exercise 4

In the same file, find the chunk of code beginning with `"vscode": {` copy and paste this chunk, ending with the bracket which closes the starting bracket on the first line.


```{r devcontainers-4}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

Your answer should look like this:

```
"vscode": {
      "extensions": [
        "ms-toolsai.jupyter",
        "ms-python.python"
      ]
    }
```

###

This section of code specifies which VS Code extensions to pre-install. `ms-toolsai.jupyter` adds support for running Jupyter notebooks directly in VS Code, while `ms-python.python` provides Python language features like code completion and error highlighting. In contrast, the `codespace-starter` template installs extensions that help with R and running the course tutorials.

VS Code is highly customizable. Personalized settings are often specified with configuration files like `devcontainers.json`.

### Exercise 5

Find the line beginning with "updateContentCommand." Copy and paste.

```{r devcontainers-5}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

Your answer should look like this:

```
"updateContentCommand": "python3 -m pip install -r requirements.txt",
```

###

`updateContentCommand` specifies a shell command that runs automatically each time the Codespace's contents are updated --- for example, when the repo changes. Here, `python3 -m pip install -r requirements.txt` reads the project's `requirements.txt` file and installs all the listed Python packages, ensuring the environment stays in sync with the project's dependencies.

### Exercise 6

Navigate to `requirements.txt` in the File Explorer. Copy and paste the entire file.

```{r devcontainers-6}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

Your answer should look like:

```
ipywidgets==8.1.8
matplotlib==3.8.4
pandas==2.2.2
torch==2.8.0
torchvision==0.23.0
tqdm==4.66.4
pillow>=12.1.1
fonttools>=4.60.0
filelock>=3.20.3
Pygments==2.20.0
```

###

The two core data science packages here are `pandas` (tabular data manipulation) and `matplotlib` (plotting and visualization) --- the Python equivalents of the tidyverse tools you've been using in R. The file also includes `torch`, a deep learning framework, which is common in Python data science environments.

### Exercise 7

Back in `devcontainer.json` copy and paste the line beginning with "postCreateCommand."

```{r devcontainers-7}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

Your answer should look like this:

```
"postCreateCommand": "",
```

###

`postCreateCommand` specifies a shell command that runs once after the Codespace is first created --- unlike `updateContentCommand`, which reruns on every update. An empty string (`""`) means no command is configured here; a project that needed a database initialized, for example, might set this to something like `"python3 setup_db.py"`.

### Exercise 8

Use AI to update the devcontainer so it installs and configures the environment for using R instead of Python. Some ways you can do this include:

1. Use the Copilot chat sidebar.

2. Copy and paste the entire document to an AI chatbot.

3. Use the gemini CLI in a bash Terminal.

Copy and paste your new `devcontainer.json`.

```{r devcontainers-8}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

My new file looks like this:

```
{
  "image": "mcr.microsoft.com/devcontainers/universal:2",
  "hostRequirements": {
    "cpus": 4
  },
  "waitFor": "onCreateCommand",
  "updateContentCommand": "Rscript -e \"if (!requireNamespace('remotes', quietly=TRUE)) install.packages('remotes', repos='https://cloud.r-project.org')\"",
  "postCreateCommand": "",
  "customizations": {
    "codespaces": {
      "openFiles": []
    },
    "vscode": {
        "extensions": [
        "ms-toolsai.jupyter",
        "Ikuyadeu.r"
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

###

Describing what you want in plain English and letting AI handle the translation is a practical use of AI for infrastructure work. The same skill you use to write analysis code with AI --- describing a goal and reviewing the output --- applies equally to configuration files like `devcontainer.json`. Once you're comfortable editing devcontainers, you can publish your own repo as a template and use it as the starting point for any future project.

### Exercise 9

Since the rest of this Codespace still uses Python, let's revert any changes we've made to `devcontainer.json`. Go to the Source Control side bar and press the revert button on the right.

```{r}
include_graphics("images/revert-devcontainer.png")
```

Run `git status` in a Bash Terminal. CP/CR.

```{r devcontainers-9}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

You should see something like this:

```
@ppbds-student ➜ /workspaces/codespaces-jupyter (main) $ git status
On branch main
nothing to commit, working tree clean
@ppbds-student ➜ /workspaces/codespaces-jupyter (main) $
```

This indicates that Git doesn't detect any changes to the repository.

###

Until you commit, changes exist only in your local working tree and can be discarded at any time. The revert button works here precisely because `devcontainer.json` was never staged or committed --- once a change is committed, undoing it requires a new commit rather than a simple discard.


### Exercise 10

In the File Explorer, select `README.md`. Copy and paste the header and first two paragraphs.

```{r devcontainers-10}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

A `README.md` is the standard introductory file for a repository --- it tells visitors what the project does, how to set it up, and how to use it. The `.md` extension stands for Markdown, a lightweight formatting syntax that uses plain-text symbols (like `#` for headers and `**` for bold) to produce readable, styled output when rendered on GitHub or in VS Code.

### Exercise 11

Replace the first paragraph with:

```
This is YOUR NAME's new python Codespace!
```

Save the changes. In the Source Control side bar, write a commit message like "Updated README". Then, press the "Commit Button". A pop-up will appear:

```{r}
include_graphics("images/commit-pop-up.png")
```

Press "Yes".

Run `git log --oneline` to view the Git history. CP/CR.

```{r devcontainers-11}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

Your output should look something like this:

```
@ppbds-student ➜ /workspaces/codespaces-jupyter (main) $ git log --oneline
bf71a79 (HEAD -> main) Updated README
7c6c35a Initial commit
@ppbds-student ➜ /workspaces/codespaces-jupyter (main) $
```

###

This pop-up doesn't appear when we use Codespaces started with `codespace-starter` because that template enables smart commit, a VS Code feature that automatically stages all tracked changes when the "Commit" button is pressed. The pop-up appeared in this Codespace because the devcontainer.json does not enable this feature.

### Exercise 12

Let's continue our tour of this repo. Navigate to `.gitignore` in the File Explorer.

Copy and paste the entire file.

```{r devcontainers-12}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

It should look something like this:

```
notebooks/data
notebooks/cifar_net.pth
.ipynb_checkpoints/
```

###

Each line excludes files that shouldn't be tracked in Git:

- `notebooks/data` ignores the data directory (which may contain large files unsuitable for GitHub)

- `notebooks/cifar_net.pth` ignores a saved PyTorch model file (also large and reproducible from training)

- `.ipynb_checkpoints/` ignores the auto-save folder that Jupyter creates whenever you run a notebook --- since those checkpoints are local scratch files, not meaningful project history.

###

### Exercise 13

Navigate to the `data` directory and open atlantis.csv. Paste the top 5 lines.

```{r devcontainers-13}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

Your answer should look something like this.

```
year,population
2000,12400
2001,12800
2002,13800
2003,13600
```

###

CSV (comma-separated values) is a plain-text format where each row is a record and each column is separated by a comma --- `atlantis.csv` contains simulated year-by-year population data for a fictional city.

### Exercise 14

Navigate to the `notebooks` directory, then `populations.ipynb`. Double click the heading of the document to open the editor for that section. Copy and paste all of the text.

```{r devcontainers-14}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

Your answer should look something like this:

```
# Population Data from CSV

This notebooks reads sample population data from `data/atlantis.csv` and plots it using Matplotlib. Edit `data/atlantis.csv` and re-run this cell to see how the plots change!
```

###

A `.ipynb` file is a Jupyter notebook --- a document that interleaves text, code, and output in a series of runnable cells, similar to a Quarto document.

`populations.ipynb` performs a simple analysis by reading the year-and-population data from `atlantis.csv` using pandas, then plotting it with matplotlib --- demonstrating the same read-then-visualize workflow you've used in R, but in Python.

### Exercise 15

Go to the Source Control side bar and press the "Publish Branch" button.

You may be asked to sign into GitHub. If so, select "Yes" then your GitHub account. This happens because we created this Codespace directly from a template. We have a local Git repository in the Codespace, but do not yet have a Git Repository on GitHub. Press the "Publish Branch" button again.

Run `git log --oneline`. CP/CR.

Now you can name the GitHub repository and then select if you'd like to make it private or public. Name it `codespaces-jupyter` and choose public.

```{r}
include_graphics("images/publish-cs.png")
```


```{r devcontainers-15}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

Your log should look like this:

```
@ppbds-student ➜ /workspaces/codespaces-jupyter (main) $ git log --oneline
bf71a79 (HEAD -> main, origin/main) Updated README
7c6c35a Initial commit
@ppbds-student ➜ /workspaces/codespaces-jupyter (main) $
```

Also notice how the Graph section of the Source Control side bar shows the same information.

```{r}
include_graphics("images/sc-graph.png")
```


###

In the `git log --oneline` output, `(HEAD -> main, origin/main)` on the same commit means your local branch and the remote are perfectly in sync. The Source Control graph shows the same thing visually --- a purple cloud icon on the commit indicates it has been pushed to the remote. Both are telling you the same story; the graph just makes it easier to see at a glance.

### Exercise 16

Go to GitHub. In the upper-right corner, click your profile picture, then select "Your repositories". Click the repository named `codespaces-jupyter`. Paste the URL of the repo.


```{r devcontainers-16}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

Your URL should look something like this:

```
https://github.com/ppbds-student/codespaces-jupyter
```
###

Codespaces started from `codespace-starter` are backed by an existing GitHub repo, so pushing just sends commits to a remote that already exists. This Codespace was created directly from a template, so there was no GitHub repo yet --- "Publish Branch" is what creates it.

## Summary
###

This tutorial explored devcontainers as a way to define and share reproducible development environments. You examined a `devcontainer.json` file in depth --- its base image, VS Code extensions, and lifecycle commands --- and compared two Codespace environments side by side: the R-focused `codespace-starter` and the Python-focused Jupyter template.

### Exercise 1

In the `09-infrastructure` Codespace, run `cat .devcontainer/devcontainer.json` in the bash Terminal. CP/CR.

```{r summary-1}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 20)
```

###

Your output should show the full contents of the `devcontainer.json` from `codespace-starter`, including fields like `image`, `features`, `customizations`, and any lifecycle commands. Compare this to the Jupyter template's `devcontainer.json` you explored in the Devcontainers section --- the two files use the same structure but configure completely different environments.

### Exercise 2

Go to https://github.com/codespaces. Stop any Codespaces you are no longer using by clicking the three-dot menu next to each one and selecting "Stop codespace". Paste the URL of your `codespaces-jupyter` GitHub repository below.

```{r summary-2}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 3)
```

###

Your URL should look something like:

```
https://github.com/ppbds-student/codespaces-jupyter
```

Stopping Codespaces when you are done with them conserves your free monthly compute hours. Deleted Codespaces cannot be recovered, but stopped ones can be restarted at any time.

### Exercise 3

Commit and push any remaining uncommitted files in your `09-infrastructure` Codespace. Copy/paste the URL to your `09-infrastructure` GitHub repo.

```{r summary-3}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 3)
```

###

The `devcontainer.json` file is itself committed to the repository --- which means the environment definition travels with the code. Anyone who forks your repo and opens a Codespace gets the exact same setup you designed. This is the same principle behind `renv.lock` for R packages or `requirements.txt` for Python: encode your environment in version-controlled files so it can be reproduced by anyone, anywhere.

```{r download-answers, child = system.file("child_documents/download_answers.Rmd", package = "tutorial.helpers")}
```
